title: Abaca fibre sheet forming
subtitle: Pulling strong, translucent sheets from abaca (Manila hemp) fibre
excerpt: Abaca, derived from the stalks of Musa textilis, produces one of the strongest natural paper fibres available to hand papermakers. Abaca sheets drain faster than cotton, form thinner, and dry to a translucent finish that makes them ideal for conservation mending tissue and for printmaking paper that needs both strength and delicacy.
sourceNotes: Abaca fibre papermaking is documented in conservation and hand-papermaking literature from the 1980s onward. No pre-1928 public-domain source covers this specific fibre in the hand-papermaking context. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation consistent with Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923, public domain) as a foundational reference for fibre preparation principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A plant fibre (Musa textilis) from the leaf stems of a relative of the banana. The long, strong fibres are used in hand papermaking and in conservation tissue.
The distribution of fibres across a sheet of handmade paper, visible when held up to the light. Even formation shows no thick or thin areas; uneven formation shows as blotches or cloudiness.
The slurry of fibre and water in the vat ready for sheet-pulling.

content.text: Abaca

fibre comes from the stalks of Musa textilis, a plant native to the Philippines and grown commercially in Asia. It is sold to hand papermakers as a prepared dry half-stuff, similar in form to cotton linter sheets. Soaked and blended to a slurry, it produces a
furnish
that drains very quickly through the mould, which means the sheet-pulling motion needs to be decisive. The resulting sheet has excellent
formation
when prepared correctly.
Keep the vat blender unplugged when adding or removing fibre. Drain the vat before reaching in with your hands. Treat the blender as any workshop machine near water.
What you need
Abaca fibre: prepared half-stuff. Soak in water for 20 minutes before blending. Break into small pieces (about 50 mm square) before soaking.
Ratio: for a fine sheet at approximately 40 gsm, use about 30 g of dry abaca fibre per 15 litres of vat water.
Optional: a small amount of methyl cellulose added to the vat slows drainage slightly and improves formation in very thin sheets.
Preparing the fibre
Soak the prepared dry abaca in cold water for 20 minutes.
Place a small handful of soaked fibre in a blender with 1 litre of water. Blend for 30 seconds. The fibre should produce a smooth, milky slurry with no long strands visible.
Add the blended slurry to the vat of water. Stir to distribute the fibre evenly. The vat water should appear lightly cloudy, not thick or opaque.
Pulling the sheet
Stir the vat immediately before pulling each sheet; abaca fibres settle faster than cotton linter.
Hold the mould and deckle together. Dip at 45 degrees into the far end of the vat, lower to horizontal below the surface, and draw toward you in one smooth motion.
Lift the mould level out of the vat. Tilt slightly to drain one corner, then hold level. Abaca drains notably faster than cotton: the water should stop running within about 15 seconds.
Remove the deckle. Couch the wet sheet onto a damp felt in the usual way.
Drying

Press and air-dry in the same way as cotton linter sheets. Abaca sheets are less prone to cockle than cotton because the fibre contracts less during drying. Thin abaca tissue (under 20 gsm) dries very flat if dried on smooth glass or acrylic sheeting.
Variations
Blend abaca with cotton linter at a 1:3 ratio for a hybrid sheet that has more body than pure abaca but better strength than pure cotton. The blend handles botanical inclusions well.
